evidence_rationale: The passage says the matters of faith contain distinct articles fitting together as parts.
evidence_text: iv) that an article is composed of words each pronounced singly and separately, thus: "Your passion, your voice, your look, have struck terror into your foes." Hence matters of Christian faith are said to contain distinct articles, in so far as they are divided into parts, and fit together

evidence_rationale: The passage explicitly concludes that charity is the friendship of man for God.
evidence_text: Accordingly, since there is a communication between man and God , inasmuch as He communicates His happiness to us, some kind of friendship must needs be based on this same communication, of which it is written ( 1 Corinthians 1:9 ): " God is faithful: by Whom you are called unto the fellowship of His Son." The love which is based on this communication, is charity : wherefore it is evident that charity is the friendship of man for God .

evidence_rationale: The respondeo explicitly concludes that charity is a virtue.
evidence_text: Wherefore, it follows that charity is a virtue , for, since charity attains God , it unites us to God , as evidenced by the authority of Augustine quoted above.

evidence_rationale: The passage explicitly says that without grace man cannot merit everlasting life.
evidence_text: And thus without grace man cannot merit everlasting life

evidence_rationale: The formal aspect of faith's object is explicitly said to be the First Truth.
evidence_text: Accordingly if we consider, in faith , the formal aspect of the object, it is nothing else than the First Truth

evidence_rationale: False prudence is presented as a likeness set against prudence simply so called.
evidence_text: There is a false prudence , which takes its name from its likeness to true prudence

evidence_rationale: The passage explicitly says justice is called a general virtue insofar as it directs to the common good.
evidence_text: It is in this sense that justice is called a general virtue

evidence_rationale: The passage explicitly defines the proper act of justice as rendering to each one his own.
evidence_text: Therefore the proper act of justice is nothing else than to render to each one his own.

evidence_rationale: The passage explicitly concludes that justice is in the will as its subject.
evidence_text: But since we are said to be just through doing something aright, and because the proximate principle of action is the appetitive power, justice must needs be in some appetitive power as its subject

evidence_rationale: The passage treats justice as a human virtue rendering act and agent good.
evidence_text: A human virtue is one "which renders a human act and man himself good " [Ethic

evidence_rationale: The passage explicitly concludes that every law is ordained to the common good.
evidence_text: Therefore every law is ordained to the common good .

evidence_rationale: The passage explicitly says law is something pertaining to reason.
evidence_text: Consequently it follows that law is something pertaining to reason .

evidence_rationale: The passage explicitly states that promulgation is necessary for law to obtain its force.
evidence_text: Wherefore promulgation is necessary for the law to obtain its force

evidence_rationale: The passage explicitly says there is need for particular justice besides legal justice.
evidence_text: Accordingly, just as in addition to legal justice there is a need for particular virtues to direct man in relation to himself, such as temperance and fortitude , so too besides legal justice there is need for particular justice to direct man in his relations to other individuals .

evidence_rationale: The passage explicitly places passion properly in the sensitive appetite.
evidence_text: This corporeal transmutation is found in the act of the sensitive appetite , and is not only spiritual , as in the sensitive apprehension, but also natural

evidence_rationale: The passage explicitly says prophecy first and chiefly consists in knowledge.
evidence_text: Prophecy first and chiefly consists in knowledge , because, to wit, prophets know things that are far [procul] removed from man's knowledge

evidence_rationale: The passage explicitly says prudence regards not only private good but also the common good.
evidence_text: Accordingly, since it belongs to prudence rightly to counsel, judge, and command concerning the means of obtaining a due end, it is evident that prudence regards not only the private good of the individual , but also the common good of the multitude.

evidence_rationale: The passage explicitly says command is the chief act of prudence.
evidence_text: Hence that which is the chief act of reason in regard to action must needs be the chief act of prudence

evidence_rationale: The passage explicitly says solicitude belongs properly to prudence.
evidence_text: vi, 9) that "one should be quick in carrying out the counsel taken, but slow in taking counsel." Hence it is that solicitude belongs properly to prudence , and for this reason Augustine says (De Morib

evidence_rationale: Prudence of the flesh is named as the prudence placing ultimate end in bodily pleasure.
evidence_text: There is a false prudence , which takes its name from its likeness to true prudence

evidence_rationale: The passage explicitly limits prudence to the practical reason.
evidence_text: Hence it is evident that prudence resides only in the practical reason.

evidence_rationale: The passage argues that prudence belongs to the cognitive faculty rather than the appetitive power.
evidence_text: x): "A prudent man is one who sees as it were from afar, for his sight is keen, and he foresees the event of uncertainties." Now sight belongs not to the appetitive but to the cognitive faculty

evidence_rationale: The passage explicitly concludes that prudence has the nature of virtue.
evidence_text: Hence prudence has the nature of virtue not only as the other intellectual virtues have it, but also as the moral virtues have it, among which virtues it is enumerated.

evidence_rationale: The Augustinian definition explicitly says sin is contrary to the eternal law.
evidence_text: the other, pertaining to the nature of evil , and which is the form, as it were, of sin , when he says, "contrary to the eternal law ."

evidence_rationale: The symbol is defined as a collection of maxims of faith so that truth may be proposed for belief.
evidence_text: It is from its being a collection of maxims of faith that the symbol [The Greek symballein ] takes its name.

evidence_rationale: The passage lists vice among the things found contrary to virtue.
evidence_text: Accordingly three things are found to be contrary to virtue

evidence_rationale: The passage says charity gives form to the acts of the virtues by directing them to the last end.
evidence_text: Now it is evident, in accordance with what has been said ( Article 7 ), that it is charity which directs the acts of all other virtues to the last end, and which, consequently, also gives the form to all other acts of virtue : and it is precisely in this sense that charity is called the form of the virtues , for these are called virtues in relation to "informed" acts.

evidence_rationale: The respondeo explicitly concludes that human virtues are habits.
evidence_text: Therefore human virtues are habits .

evidence_rationale: The respondeo explicitly concludes that human virtue is an operative habit.
evidence_text: Consequently it is essential to human virtue to be an operative habit .
